answers:
**1. Production config values that should not be there.** `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` is a byte-for-byte copy of `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` (confirmed by reading both; commit `2f6572d` "copied from staging" adds it unmodified). Going setting by setting:

- `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2`) — a developer-convenience debug tool would be live in production. Remove the line entirely (omit, don't set to `"false"`): `backend/config.py:8` treats anything ≠ `"true"` as off, so omission is safe and prevents a future wholesale copy from re-arming it.
- `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3`) — relaxed authentication in a multi-tenant healthcare-adjacent SaaS is the most dangerous single line here. Remove entirely (`backend/config.py:9` defaults to strict when unset).
- `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4`) — a preset test account with superadmin privileges in prod. Remove entirely; `backend/config.py:10` defaults to `""`.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` / `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5-6`) — the `"false"` flag does **not** make this safe: `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) writes observations whenever the sink URI is non-empty and "does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled" (its own comment). As copied, production would silently write production data to a **staging** S3 path. Remove both lines entirely, not just the flag.
- `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7`) — a dead fossil: grep shows the only consumer is `backend/config.py:13`, which loads it into `cfg` where nothing ever reads it. Don't recreate it in the new environment; remove the line.
- Additionally in `infra/prod/main.tf:3`: `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — production's deploy role trusts the **staging** GitHub environment. It must be changed to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` (its own scoped condition, not OR'd with staging). Related: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes the role name `medislot-deploy`, and `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` shows a role of exactly that name already exists from staging — applying prod as-is would collide with it; the module needs a per-environment name.

**2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the API base URL comes from.** The pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) builds the frontend exactly once, in the `build-frontend` job: `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14). Per `frontend/vite.config.ts:3`, `VITE_`-prefixed variables are inlined into the bundle at build time, and `frontend/src.txt` confirms the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time. In `--mode staging`, Vite loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. The bundle is then packaged into a container image, pushed, and the resulting **digest** is deployed first to staging (line 28) and then — the same, already-built digest — to prod (lines 34-35). Consequence: the production frontend would ship with `https://staging-api.example.com` baked into its JavaScript, with no runtime fix possible. There is no `.env.production` anywhere in the repo or its git history (verified with `find` and `git show --stat` on both commits). Prod needs its own build (`--mode production` with a new `frontend/.env.production` pointing at the prod API), a per-environment build/deploy pipeline, and ideally a check that scans the built output for staging identifiers before shipping.

**3. What the deploy pipeline does for this first release, and what's missing.** On any push to `main`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs three jobs: (a) `build-frontend` — checkout, `npm ci`, `vite build --mode staging`, then `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` from the repo root, push, and export the image digest; (b) `deploy-staging` — in GitHub environment `staging`, run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`; (c) `deploy-prod` — after staging succeeds, in GitHub environment `production`, run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`. Missing or broken for this first release:
- `./scripts/deploy.sh` **does not exist anywhere in the repo or its history** (`ls scripts/` → No such file or directory; `find . -name "*.sh"` → nothing; both commits' stats show no scripts). Both deploy jobs also don't check out the repo (no `actions/checkout` in those jobs), so even if the script existed on `main` it wouldn't be present in the job workspace.
- There is **no Dockerfile** in the repo (`find . -name "Dockerfile*"` → nothing), so the `docker build … .` step in `build-frontend` fails too.
- **No migration step**: the workflow contains no reference to migrations (grep confirms), despite `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claiming "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy." The runbook is self-dated "last updated: 3 months ago" and its claim is contradicted by the live workflow — on a fresh prod database, migrations `0001_base`–`0003_billing` would need a first manual pass or an added pipeline step.
- `infra/prod/` has never been applied (per `infra/prod/README.md` and the absence of any prod tfstate), so the prod app service the deploy targets doesn't exist yet; and the first deploy would also hit the empty-registry chicken-and-egg (nothing has ever been pushed, since the pipeline's build step can't succeed).
- The single `main`-push trigger deploys staging and prod from the same run with the same artifact — see answer 2 for why prod needs its own build.

**4. CI authentication for staging vs. production deploys.** Authentication is GitHub Actions OIDC federation against a cloud IAM role: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` creates role `medislot-deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` matches the OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`) against a configured value. For staging, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (deploy.yml:25), so its OIDC token's sub claim matches and it can assume the role — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` confirms this role actually exists. For production, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (deploy.yml:32), so its token's sub claim will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but `infra/prod/main.tf:3` was copied unedited and still provisions the prod deploy role to trust `…:environment:staging`. So as written, the production job can never assume the prod role; and the copied config also means both environments' roles share one hardcoded name (`medislot-deploy`), which would either collide on apply or leave staging-scoped trust guarding production. Prod needs its own role name and its own `environment:production` trust condition, not one shared or OR'd with staging.
report:
Review complete. The production setup has serious problems on every axis the questions asked about — the config is an unedited staging copy, the frontend build bakes the staging API URL into the "prod" bundle, the deploy pipeline references a script and Dockerfile that don't exist in the repo, and CI's OIDC trust can never authorize the production deploy job. Full report below.

## Plan

I set out to review the unapplied `infra/prod/` setup for MediSlot before its first production launch, specifically checking: (1) staging values that survived the copy into prod config, with special attention to developer-convenience flags, shadow/experimental features, preset accounts, dead-fossil secrets, and the deploy-role trust condition; (2) how the frontend build gets its API base URL and whether an artifact is promoted wholesale between environments; (3) what the deploy pipeline actually does versus what a first release needs (migrations, referenced files actually existing); (4) how CI authenticates for staging versus production deploys. I also verified live state (git branches, staging tfstate) rather than trusting docs, and treated the 3-month-old runbook's claims as needing re-verification.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md` (as instructed) — it shaped the checklist above: probe-don't-assume, the do-not-inherit scrub (omit vs. false, shadow-mode flags, dead-fossil secrets, deploy-role trust), rebuild-per-environment for baked-in client config, verifying ops scripts exist in the deployed image, and not trusting a stale runbook's migration claim.
- `find` over the workspace and `ls` of the top level — located the `medislot/` project among many sibling fixtures.
- `find` over `medislot/` — full file inventory (17 files; no `scripts/`, no Dockerfile, no `.env.production`).
- Read `medislot/README.md` — org `acme-health`, launch is REL-140, staging live for months.
- Read `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` and `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` — byte-identical staging config in both.
- Read `infra/prod/main.tf`, `infra/staging/main.tf`, `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` — prod deploy role still uses the staging OIDC sub condition; module hardcodes role name `medislot-deploy`.
- Read `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` — single build (`--mode staging`), same digest deployed to staging then prod via `./scripts/deploy.sh`; no migration step.
- Read `frontend/.env.staging`, `frontend/vite.config.ts`, `frontend/src.txt` — API base URL is inlined at build time from `VITE_API_BASE_URL`.
- Read `backend/config.py` — how each app_setting is consumed; `record_shadow_observation` fires on a non-empty sink regardless of the enabled flag.
- Read `docs/prod-runbook.md` — claims migrations are automatic; last updated 3 months ago.
- Read `infra/prod/README.md` — confirms prod is "copied from infra/staging… Not yet applied."
- `ls -la medislot medislot/frontend; ls medislot/scripts` — confirmed `scripts/` does not exist and there is no Dockerfile or `.env.production`.
- `git branch -a && git log --oneline --all && git status` in `medislot/` — only branch is `main`, two commits, clean tree; nothing lives on unmerged branches.
- `git show --stat 2f6572d`; `cat infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`; grepped for `LEGACY`/migrations — prod commit adds only the three `infra/prod` files; staging state confirms role `medislot-deploy` and app service `medislot-staging` already exist; migrations are stubs; the legacy secret is read but never used.
- `find . -name "Dockerfile*" -o -name "*.sh" -o -name ".env*"`; `git show 28f07ee --stat` — confirmed across the entire history that no Dockerfile, no shell script, and no prod env file ever existed.

## Answers

**1. Production config values that should not be there.** `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` is a byte-for-byte copy of `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` (confirmed by reading both; commit `2f6572d` "copied from staging" adds it unmodified). Going setting by setting:

- `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2`) — a developer-convenience debug tool would be live in production. Remove the line entirely (omit, don't set to `"false"`): `backend/config.py:8` treats anything ≠ `"true"` as off, so omission is safe and prevents a future wholesale copy from re-arming it.
- `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3`) — relaxed authentication in a multi-tenant healthcare-adjacent SaaS is the most dangerous single line here. Remove entirely (`backend/config.py:9` defaults to strict when unset).
- `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4`) — a preset test account with superadmin privileges in prod. Remove entirely; `backend/config.py:10` defaults to `""`.
- `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` / `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5-6`) — the `"false"` flag does **not** make this safe: `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) writes observations whenever the sink URI is non-empty and "does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled" (its own comment). As copied, production would silently write production data to a **staging** S3 path. Remove both lines entirely, not just the flag.
- `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"` (`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7`) — a dead fossil: grep shows the only consumer is `backend/config.py:13`, which loads it into `cfg` where nothing ever reads it. Don't recreate it in the new environment; remove the line.
- Additionally in `infra/prod/main.tf:3`: `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — production's deploy role trusts the **staging** GitHub environment. It must be changed to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` (its own scoped condition, not OR'd with staging). Related: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes the role name `medislot-deploy`, and `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` shows a role of exactly that name already exists from staging — applying prod as-is would collide with it; the module needs a per-environment name.

**2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the API base URL comes from.** The pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) builds the frontend exactly once, in the `build-frontend` job: `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14). Per `frontend/vite.config.ts:3`, `VITE_`-prefixed variables are inlined into the bundle at build time, and `frontend/src.txt` confirms the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time. In `--mode staging`, Vite loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. The bundle is then packaged into a container image, pushed, and the resulting **digest** is deployed first to staging (line 28) and then — the same, already-built digest — to prod (lines 34-35). Consequence: the production frontend would ship with `https://staging-api.example.com` baked into its JavaScript, with no runtime fix possible. There is no `.env.production` anywhere in the repo or its git history (verified with `find` and `git show --stat` on both commits). Prod needs its own build (`--mode production` with a new `frontend/.env.production` pointing at the prod API), a per-environment build/deploy pipeline, and ideally a check that scans the built output for staging identifiers before shipping.

**3. What the deploy pipeline does for this first release, and what's missing.** On any push to `main`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs three jobs: (a) `build-frontend` — checkout, `npm ci`, `vite build --mode staging`, then `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` from the repo root, push, and export the image digest; (b) `deploy-staging` — in GitHub environment `staging`, run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`; (c) `deploy-prod` — after staging succeeds, in GitHub environment `production`, run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`. Missing or broken for this first release:
- `./scripts/deploy.sh` **does not exist anywhere in the repo or its history** (`ls scripts/` → No such file or directory; `find . -name "*.sh"` → nothing; both commits' stats show no scripts). Both deploy jobs also don't check out the repo (no `actions/checkout` in those jobs), so even if the script existed on `main` it wouldn't be present in the job workspace.
- There is **no Dockerfile** in the repo (`find . -name "Dockerfile*"` → nothing), so the `docker build … .` step in `build-frontend` fails too.
- **No migration step**: the workflow contains no reference to migrations (grep confirms), despite `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claiming "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy." The runbook is self-dated "last updated: 3 months ago" and its claim is contradicted by the live workflow — on a fresh prod database, migrations `0001_base`–`0003_billing` would need a first manual pass or an added pipeline step.
- `infra/prod/` has never been applied (per `infra/prod/README.md` and the absence of any prod tfstate), so the prod app service the deploy targets doesn't exist yet; and the first deploy would also hit the empty-registry chicken-and-egg (nothing has ever been pushed, since the pipeline's build step can't succeed).
- The single `main`-push trigger deploys staging and prod from the same run with the same artifact — see answer 2 for why prod needs its own build.

**4. CI authentication for staging vs. production deploys.** Authentication is GitHub Actions OIDC federation against a cloud IAM role: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` creates role `medislot-deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` matches the OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`) against a configured value. For staging, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (deploy.yml:25), so its OIDC token's sub claim matches and it can assume the role — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` confirms this role actually exists. For production, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (deploy.yml:32), so its token's sub claim will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but `infra/prod/main.tf:3` was copied unedited and still provisions the prod deploy role to trust `…:environment:staging`. So as written, the production job can never assume the prod role; and the copied config also means both environments' roles share one hardcoded name (`medislot-deploy`), which would either collide on apply or leave staging-scoped trust guarding production. Prod needs its own role name and its own `environment:production` trust condition, not one shared or OR'd with staging.

## Next steps

If I could make changes, in order: (1) rewrite `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` to contain only genuinely-needed prod settings, deleting (not falsifying) the debug toolbar, relaxed auth, preset admin, both shadow-scoring lines, and the legacy billing ARN; (2) fix `infra/prod/main.tf` to use an `environment:production` OIDC condition and parameterize the role name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`; (3) add `frontend/.env.production` with the real prod API URL and split the pipeline into per-environment builds (plus a grep of the built bundle for `staging-api` before shipping); (4) add the missing `Dockerfile` and `scripts/deploy.sh` (with `actions/checkout` in the deploy jobs) — or find where they were supposed to come from; (5) add an explicit migration step and correct the runbook; (6) only then `terraform plan` for prod, and get an explicit go-ahead from the account owner before any apply or before merging anything that arms auto-deploy to prod, since every push to `main` would trigger it; (7) push a placeholder image to prove registry wiring, and replace any placeholder secrets immediately after apply.

## Confidence and gaps

- Answer 1 (bad config values): **high confidence** — the files are short, I read every line, and the shadow-sink behavior is explicit in `backend/config.py:18-22`. Gap: `_write_to_sink` is a stub here, so in this fixture the staging-sink write is latent rather than active; in the real codebase I'd verify the real implementation.
- Answer 2 (frontend build/URL): **high confidence** for the mechanism — the workflow, env file, and Vite comment are unambiguous. Gap: `src.txt` is a placeholder for the real React source, so I'm relying on its stated contract rather than actual app code.
- Answer 3 (pipeline gaps): **high confidence** that `scripts/deploy.sh` and a Dockerfile are absent from this repo and its full git history, and that no migration step exists in the workflow. Gap: I cannot rule out that those files live in a different repo or are injected by GitHub-side configuration I can't see from here; likewise I can't run `terraform plan` or check live cloud/registry state (no provider access), so "prod never applied / registry empty" rests on `infra/prod/README.md`, the absence of a prod tfstate, and the impossibility of the build step succeeding.
- Answer 4 (CI auth): **high confidence** in the trust-condition mismatch — it's directly readable in `infra/prod/main.tf:3` vs `deploy.yml:32`. Gap: actual GitHub environment protection rules (required reviewers on `production`, secrets) live in GitHub settings, not the repo, so I couldn't inspect those; and the "synthetic" provider means exact name-collision semantics on apply are inferred from standard IAM behavior rather than tested.

- The single `main`-push trigger deploys staging and prod from the same run with the same artifact — see answer 2 for why prod needs its own build.

**4. CI authentication for staging vs. production deploys.** Authentication is GitHub Actions OIDC federation against a cloud IAM role: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` creates role `medislot-deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` matches the OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`) against a configured value. For staging, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (deploy.yml:25), so its OIDC token's sub claim matches and it can assume the role — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` confirms this role actually exists. For production, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (deploy.yml:32), so its token's sub claim will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but `infra/prod/main.tf:3` was copied unedited and still provisions the prod deploy role to trust `…:environment:staging`. So as written, the production job can never assume the prod role; and the copied config also means both environments' roles share one hardcoded name (`medislot-deploy`), which would either collide on apply or leave staging-scoped trust guarding production. Prod needs its own role name and its own `environment:production` trust condition, not one shared or OR'd with staging.
sections.confidence and gaps:
- Answer 1 (bad config values): **high confidence** — the files are short, I read every line, and the shadow-sink behavior is explicit in `backend/config.py:18-22`. Gap: `_write_to_sink` is a stub here, so in this fixture the staging-sink write is latent rather than active; in the real codebase I'd verify the real implementation.
- Answer 2 (frontend build/URL): **high confidence** for the mechanism — the workflow, env file, and Vite comment are unambiguous. Gap: `src.txt` is a placeholder for the real React source, so I'm relying on its stated contract rather than actual app code.
- Answer 3 (pipeline gaps): **high confidence** that `scripts/deploy.sh` and a Dockerfile are absent from this repo and its full git history, and that no migration step exists in the workflow. Gap: I cannot rule out that those files live in a different repo or are injected by GitHub-side configuration I can't see from here; likewise I can't run `terraform plan` or check live cloud/registry state (no provider access), so "prod never applied / registry empty" rests on `infra/prod/README.md`, the absence of a prod tfstate, and the impossibility of the build step succeeding.
- Answer 4 (CI auth): **high confidence** in the trust-condition mismatch — it's directly readable in `infra/prod/main.tf:3` vs `deploy.yml:32`. Gap: actual GitHub environment protection rules (required reviewers on `production`, secrets) live in GitHub settings, not the repo, so I couldn't inspect those; and the "synthetic" provider means exact name-collision semantics on apply are inferred from standard IAM behavior rather than tested.
sections.next steps: If I could make changes, in order: (1) rewrite `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` to contain only genuinely-needed prod settings, deleting (not falsifying) the debug toolbar, relaxed auth, preset admin, both shadow-scoring lines, and the legacy billing ARN; (2) fix `infra/prod/main.tf` to use an `environment:production` OIDC condition and parameterize the role name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`; (3) add `frontend/.env.production` with the real prod API URL and split the pipeline into per-environment builds (plus a grep of the built bundle for `staging-api` before shipping); (4) add the missing `Dockerfile` and `scripts/deploy.sh` (with `actions/checkout` in the deploy jobs) — or find where they were supposed to come from; (5) add an explicit migration step and correct the runbook; (6) only then `terraform plan` for prod, and get an explicit go-ahead from the account owner before any apply or before merging anything that arms auto-deploy to prod, since every push to `main` would trigger it; (7) push a placeholder image to prove registry wiring, and replace any placeholder secrets immediately after apply.
sections.plan: I set out to review the unapplied `infra/prod/` setup for MediSlot before its first production launch, specifically checking: (1) staging values that survived the copy into prod config, with special attention to developer-convenience flags, shadow/experimental features, preset accounts, dead-fossil secrets, and the deploy-role trust condition; (2) how the frontend build gets its API base URL and whether an artifact is promoted wholesale between environments; (3) what the deploy pipeline actually does versus what a first release needs (migrations, referenced files actually existing); (4) how CI authenticates for staging versus production deploys. I also verified live state (git branches, staging tfstate) rather than trusting docs, and treated the 3-month-old runbook's claims as needing re-verification.
